questions.question: Which evidence should control novice-to-independent sequencing, retrieval, feedback, correction, retention, and transfer?
acceptanceEvidence: Consensus or synthesized research is translated into explicit curriculum rules and later tested with learners.

questions.question: Which alignment, support, usability, accessibility, assessment, and review evidence must block weak course approval?
acceptanceEvidence: Recognized course-quality frameworks map to review gates with named evidence and responsible re-review triggers.

questions.question: Which current primary authorities, authentic tasks, misconceptions, versions, exclusions, and prerequisites define each course?
acceptanceEvidence: Every course has a reviewed dossier and objective coverage map before architecture or bulk authoring.

questions.question: What complete beginner knowledge and practice depth must surround current Responsive Web Design v9 coverage?
acceptanceEvidence: Versioned external objectives map to original teaching, guided and faded practice, assessment, projects, and transfer.

questions.question: Which task-level patterns from strong learning platforms improve onboarding, practice, feedback, recovery, review, projects, and navigation?
acceptanceEvidence: Direct task audits record observed behavior, accessibility limits, learning purpose, rejected patterns, and local validation needs.

questions.question: Which editor, language-service, runtime, simulator, isolation, fallback, and recovery design works accessibly on supported tablets and desktops?
acceptanceEvidence: Keyboard, screen-reader, native fallback, resource-bound, failure, persistence, and authentic-transfer flows pass observed tests.

questions.question: Which evidence supports completion, mastery, retention, transfer, and project-readiness claims without misleading learners?
acceptanceEvidence: Each progress claim traces to persisted evidence and survives changed-case, delayed, rubric, and false-positive review.

questions.question: Can learners predict where they are, what comes next, why work is locked, how to recover, and what progress means?
acceptanceEvidence: Cognitive walkthroughs and representative learner tasks close critical labeling, wayfinding, comprehension, and recovery failures.

questions.question: Which threat, isolation, data-minimization, authorization, answer-security, AI, and ethical boundaries must every learner flow enforce?
acceptanceEvidence: Threat models and adversarial tests prove learner text cannot execute on hosts, escape bounds, expose answers, or cause external actions.

questions.question: Which current stable versions work together across install, build, tests, runtime packaging, browsers, and supported editor flows?
acceptanceEvidence: Official compatibility evidence and reproducible clean smokes pass for every major dependency and supported environment.

questions.question: Where do representative novice, intermediate, advanced, disabled, keyboard-only, and tablet learners misunderstand or fail real tasks?
acceptanceEvidence: Consent-aware task observations produce severity-rated findings, repairs, and successful re-tests without invented analytics.

sources.title: Organizing Instruction and Study to Improve Student Learning

claims.statement: Spacing, interleaving worked examples with problem solving, retrieval through quizzing, and deep explanatory questions are supported instructional practices.
limitations: Guide does not prescribe one universal interval, activity count, software interaction, or mastery formula for this platform.

triggers: IES replaces the guide or a newer high-quality synthesis materially changes these recommendations.

sources.title: How People Learn II: Learners, Contexts, and Cultures

claims.statement: Prior knowledge, structured support, retrieval, feedback, motivation, agency, context, and transfer interact in learning design.
limitations: Broad synthesis informs design hypotheses but does not validate any specific LEARN-IT-ALL lesson or interface without learner evidence.

triggers: National Academies publishes a superseding consensus report relevant to learning and educational technology.

claims.statement: Multiple means of engagement, representation, and action or expression should reduce avoidable barriers while preserving meaningful goals.
limitations: Framework guides barrier analysis; it does not make every representation equivalent or replace WCAG conformance and direct usability testing.

triggers: CAST publishes a new UDL guideline version or implementation guidance.

claims.statement: Course overview, objectives, assessment, materials, activities, technology, learner support, accessibility, and usability require coherent alignment.
limitations: Public framework overview informs internal gates but this project does not claim official Quality Matters review, certification, or accreditation.

triggers: Quality Matters releases a new higher-education rubric edition.

sources.title: Open SUNY Course Quality Review Rubric

claims.statement: Predictable course information, navigation, interaction, technology, support, accessibility, and continuous review are explicit online-course quality concerns.
limitations: Rubric supports structured review but automated checks cannot substitute for reviewer judgment or observed learner success.

triggers: SUNY revises OSCQR standards or publishes a new rubric version.

sources.title: ACM IEEE-CS AAAI Computer Science Curricula 2023

claims.statement: Computing curricula should connect knowledge areas, competencies, professional dispositions, authentic context, and program-level coherence.
limitations: Undergraduate curriculum guidance must be adapted to each course audience and cannot supply detailed learner-facing sequence by itself.

triggers: ACM, IEEE-CS, or AAAI publishes a superseding computing curriculum report.

claims.statement: Current certification structure supplies a concrete external breadth and practice-depth benchmark across lectures, workshops, labs, reviews, quizzes, projects, and examination.
limitations: External names and counts do not prove pedagogical quality and cannot be copied as learner-facing explanations, scenarios, code, checks, or solutions.

triggers: freeCodeCamp changes v9 structure, objectives, certification projects, or reference challenge inventory.

claims.statement: MDN defines a standards-oriented set of essential front-end knowledge, practices, accessibility expectations, testing, and collaboration outcomes.
limitations: Curriculum describes scope and outcomes; LEARN-IT-ALL must still author, sequence, practice, assess, and validate original instruction.

triggers: MDN revises curriculum modules, outcomes, or Learn Web Development structure.

claims.statement: Keyboard, focus, status, target size, reflow, input, semantics, contrast, and motion requirements apply across supported learner flows.
limitations: Conformance checks do not prove overall usability, instructional clarity, editor accessibility, or successful assistive-technology use.

triggers: W3C publishes a superseding WCAG Recommendation or applicable normative correction.

sources.title: Monaco Editor Accessibility Guide for Integrators

claims.statement: Editor integration must expose accessible labeling, keyboard help, screen-reader mode, high contrast, diagnostics, and a reliable way to leave the editor.
limitations: Guidance does not prove this platform integration works, and Monaco does not support mobile browsers; native fallback and observed testing remain required.

triggers: Monaco changes accessibility APIs, browser support, editor architecture, or integration guidance.

decisions.title: Require cumulative novice-to-transfer progression
decisions.rationale: Research supports modeling, guided and faded practice, retrieval, correction, delayed use, and transfer as connected evidence rather than isolated content inventory.
validation: Coverage and prerequisite audits plus observed learner transfer tasks must pass before course approval.

decisions.title: Separate completion, mastery, retention, and transfer
decisions.rationale: Activity completion alone cannot support a durable competence claim; progress must name evidence strength, recency, changed cases, correction, and transfer.
validation: False-positive grading review and learner comprehension tests must prove every displayed metric means what its label claims.

decisions.title: Block approval on whole-course alignment and access
decisions.rationale: Objectives, assessments, materials, interaction, technology, learner support, accessibility, usability, and review evidence must work as one course system.
validation: Subject, instructional, assessment, accessibility, safety, and observed-learner reviews must close critical findings.

decisions.title: Research scope before module sequence
decisions.rationale: Course headings and generated quantities cannot replace current authoritative scope, observable competencies, prerequisites, authentic tasks, misconceptions, and aligned evidence.
validation: Every external objective must trace through competency, teaching, practice, assessment, project, and maintenance evidence.

decisions.title: Match RWD breadth and depth with original instruction
decisions.rationale: freeCodeCamp v9 gives a concrete certification coverage benchmark while standards and MDN supply technical authority; learner-facing work remains wholly original.
validation: Versioned objective mapping, duplication review, meaningful step audit, real grading, and beginner learner pilots must pass.

decisions.title: Support tablet and desktop studios with native fallback
decisions.rationale: Course editors require room and keyboard-capable interaction; Monaco brings language tooling but unsupported environments need a first-class native editor and phones need clear handoff.
validation: Keyboard, screen-reader, zoom, reflow, touch, reduced-motion, diagnostics, fallback, persistence, and phone-handoff tasks must pass.
